topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias traced the ink on the worn paperback, his knuckles white against the spine. The words, the very rhythm of his imagination, felt suddenly contaminated. He had adored Julian Vance’s prose—the intricate world-building, the aching emotional resonance. Now, the realization hit him like a physical blow: Vance hadn't just borrowed; he had lifted entire sequences, entire thematic arcs, from Elias’s unpublished manuscripts. A cold, sharp pressure built behind Elias’s ribs, tightening his chest until breathing became a shallow, ragged thing. He slammed the book shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. He didn't look at the shelf of Vance’s works; he just stared at the blank wall, his jaw clenched so tight he tasted copper. Every thought that followed was laced with a burning resentment, a corrosive feeling that demanded retribution, even if the only thing he could manage was silent, furious pacing.

[story 2]

"It's all mine." The words were a low rasp in Maya’s throat, barely audible over the clatter of the coffee machine. She stared at the screen, the meticulously crafted dialogue of her novel laid bare next to Vance’s published chapter. The familiarity of the phrasing, the unique metaphors she had poured months into developing, suddenly felt like a grotesque mockery. A tremor ran through her hands, causing her to grip the edge of the desk until her nails dug crescents into her palms. She pushed back from the computer, the chair scraping violently against the hardwood floor. Her breath hitched, sharp and quick. She walked to the window, the city lights blurring into streaks of meaningless color. A profound sense of injustice settled over her, heavy and suffocating, making her skin feel too tight, too hot. She didn't want to scream; she just wanted to dismantle everything Vance had built, brick by agonizing brick.

[story 3]

When the editor forwarded the comparison, Liam felt the blood drain from his face. He reread the excerpt, his eyes scanning the familiar sentence structures, the signature cadence of his own internal narrative. A slow, simmering rage began to boil beneath his calm exterior. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over Vance’s social media profile. The sheer audacity of the theft made his stomach churn with a nauseating sickness. He didn't speak to anyone immediately. Instead, he walked into his studio, moving with a deliberate, coiled tension. He picked up a heavy, smooth stone from his desk and began to rub it repeatedly between his palms, the friction a desperate attempt to channel the surging internal storm. He felt a fierce, protective instinct rise, a deep-seated need to reclaim what was stolen, a burning desire to expose the deceit, even if it meant burning down the entire edifice Vance had constructed.
